tldr: Headlines from all 14 country buckets announce the Houthi naval blockade of Saudi Arabia as a military escalation in the US-Iran war. A parallel set of East Asian and Western explainer titles foregrounds the global energy supply threat, while only Iranian state media and an Indian nationalist outlet lead with the 'eye for eye' retaliation framing that most Western headlines relegate to the body text.

frames.sub_frame: Naval blockade: new Red Sea war front

evidence.quote: threatening new front in US-Iran war
Houthis declare naval blockade of Saudi Arabia
US continues to attack Iran as tension expands to Red Sea

frames.sub_frame: Red Sea closure threatens energy supply

evidence.quote: Could Disrupt Crude Supply to S. Korea
tightens Iran's grip on global energy supply
tightens Iran's grip on global energy supplies

frames.sub_frame: An eye for eye: retaliation for siege

evidence.quote: 'An Eye For An Eye': Yemen's Houthis Impose Shipping Ban

single_outlet_findings.finding: The title 'Yemeni armed forces declare full naval blockade against Saudi Arabia' uses 'Yemeni armed forces' rather than 'Houthis' — the only headline in the corpus to legitimise the blockade as a sovereign state act, a framing distinction visible before any reader clicks.

The only headline to name a specific national import dependency ('Crude Supply to S. Korea'), making the energy risk explicitly personal rather than abstract-global — a divergence from the generic 'global energy supplies' phrasing used by Japan Times, Straits Times, and Globe and Mail.

The only headline to foreground the Houthi rhetorical framing verbatim ('An Eye For An Eye') rather than the factual announcement, advertising the retaliatory logic on the homepage in a way that every other outlet reserves for the body text.

bottom_line: Headline-layer coverage advertises the Houthi naval blockade primarily as a military escalation threatening global energy supplies, with retaliatory justice framing visible only in Iranian state and Indian right-populist titles.
